column24: A floor-to-standing desk that’s manually adjustable, minimalist, and ergonomic — designed for people who alternate between floor-sitting and standing, without the high price tag that the existing 3 floor sit to stand desk options have.
column25: I’d like Generate to help design and prototype a mechanical lifting system — ideally using counterweights, gears, or pulleys — that allows for a smooth, stable, and user-controlled height adjustment between ~13" and ~47". This is the central feature of the product and key to making the desk affordable, functional, and distinct from electric-only models.
column26:
A working prototype (or partial prototype) that successfully adjusts between sitting and standing heights using a low-tech system

CAD designs and specs that I can use to iterate further or bring to a manufacturer

Insights and validation from student engineers and designers on feasibility and improvements

A clear understanding of potential production costs and material sourcing
column27:
People who work from home and alternate between floor-sitting and standing (e.g., wellness-focused, yoga/movement practitioners, and neurodiverse individuals)

Early adopters in ergonomic and minimalist home office setups

I’ve conducted informal interviews and research in niche wellness and productivity communities (e.g., Reddit, Instagram) — many users feel existing desks don’t go low enough or are overpriced

There is a gap in the market for a non-electric, cleanly designed desk with a broader height range and better price point
column28:
Uppeal Desk – Electric and well-designed, but too tall for true floor-sitting (15” minimum) and expensive (~$1,600)

Lillipad – Best low height (6”), portable and pre-assembled, but has overly governed motor and costs ~$1,000+

Limber Life Co – Manual, eco-conscious, but unstable when adjusting and priced at ~$2,900

DIY Reddit projects – Show clear interest in a hackable, affordable solution — but lack polish and usability

column24: MixedLCmediA (our venture) produces next-generation open-tubular nanoflow liquid chromatography technology that allows researchers and analytical chemists around the globe to analyze minute sample amounts and/or get more informative data for the same amount of starting material by increasing signal-to-noise and sensitivity of mass spectrometry-based proteomic profiling.
column25: During the past couple of years, we optimized the chemistry and preparation protocol of our novel liquid chromatography columns, built, tested, and benchmarked the prototype. The current prototype is bench type, meaning we know how to prepare all individual parts, but the setup is a bit “messy”. We would like Generate to help us build a cellphone-sized plug-and-play product enclosure/configuration with additional features, including temperature and voltage control, displays, run time counter, additional fluidic switching valves, etc. Some features can potentially be controlled by software like Arduino. Also, the whole setup needs to be enclosed in a case to prevent damage during transportation, handling, and use.
column26: Fully functional and operational product (or advanced-stage prototype) that can be further tested in our lab and by several of our collaborators. We have several ongoing collaborations, both academic and industrial. Testing can be performed anytime throughout the product development process.
column27:
We identified several applications and market directions that will potentially benefit from the implementation of our technology: 1) the single-cell proteomics market is rapidly emerging following the fast and impressive expansion of the single-cell transcriptomics technology market size, which is currently >$4B (that is expected to grow at a compound annual growth rate, CAGR, of ~18-25% from 2023 to 2030); 2) The market focused on the analysis of limited clinical samples, including small volumes of physiological fluids (liquid biopsies), rare cell isolates, cell-based personalized therapies, dried blood spots, and minute tissue biopsies; 3) biopharma/biotechnology-related sample types (e.g., clone selection, biotherapeutic/vaccine target delivery.

The target market is in analytical chemistry laboratories. Also, there are ~280,000 research laboratories worldwide, where ~5-10% of them might be interested in our proposed technology. However, if we focus our market sizing analysis on several sectors of the high-performance liquid chromatography product markets, the identified serviceable obtainable market (SOM) of nano liquid chromatography columns for proteomics currently corresponds to $220M with a projected CAGR of 5.9% - 12% from 2023 to 2030.

As a part of the IDEA Ready Stage and NSF I-Corps at NU, we did the initial customer discovery and product-market fit.
column28:
Based on our due diligence, our direct competitors are the vendors producing “conventional” state-of-the-art bead-packed nano liquid chromatography columns, e.g.,
-	IonOpticks
-	PepSep (acquired by Bruker Daltonics in 2021)
-	CoAnn Technologies
-	ESI Source Solutions
-	PharmaFluidics (acquired by Thermo Fisher in 2021)
 The columns produced and sold by these companies do not meet current customer needs in achieving the desired sensitivity for limited samples, and they also cannot operate at ultra-low flow. Importantly, none of these vendors specializes in producing ultra-low flow sub-20 μm internal diameter columns (porous layer open tubular, bead-packed, or monolithic), but they are trying to fill in the corresponding market space with alternative, however, suboptimal solutions that still require larger sample amounts.

column24: Recura’s smart resistance band delivers real-time feedback, seamless tracking, and personalized progress insights, empowering physical therapy patients to perform their exercises safely and effectively wherever they are.
column25:
We would like Generate to help us prototype a Bluetooth-enabled smart resistance band system that integrates real-time tension sensors and supports interchangeable bands. The prototype should include sensor calibration, embedded firmware for accurate data transmission, and seamless Bluetooth connectivity with the existing Recura iOS app. This would allow users to automatically log resistance levels, reps, and sets while receiving real-time performance feedback.

By focusing on usability and data connectivity, Generate’s support will directly enable the core value proposition of Recura by empowering physical therapy patients to track and improve their rehab exercises safely and effectively across settings.
column26:
At the end of the semester with Generate, we would consider it a success to have a fully functional smart resistance band prototype with integrated tension sensors, Bluetooth connectivity, and real-time syncing capabilities with the Recura app. This includes interchangeable resistance bands, accurate data capture for sets, reps, and resistance levels, and responsive feedback through the app interface.

Success also includes receiving a complete engineering package: CAD files, circuit schematics, embedded firmware, and BLE communication documentation to support future development. We plan to test the prototype in collaboration with our existing clinical partners—The Physio Movement in Singapore and Downtown Physical Therapy in Boston—to evaluate its real-world performance and gather feedback for refinement.

This outcome would allow us to validate the prototype’s core functionality, confirm its usability in both clinical and at-home settings, and lay a strong foundation for future iterations and broader deployment.
column27:
Our target market includes physical therapy clinics and their patients who require structured rehabilitation through resistance-based exercises. We are developing Recura’s smart resistance band as a dual-solution: a tool for clinicians to enhance patient tracking and treatment planning, and a device for patients to use at home or in the clinic for consistent, feedback-rich recovery.

We’ve begun testing product-market fit through ongoing collaboration with two clinical partners—The Physio Movement in Singapore and Downtown Physical Therapy in Boston. Through these partnerships, we've validated a strong interest in a device that can streamline exercise tracking, provide real-time feedback, and bridge the data gap between clinic-based sessions and at-home rehab.

Early conversations and testing have revealed clear pain points that Recura aims to address, such as poor exercise adherence, lack of real-time feedback on form, and difficulty tracking resistance progress over time. These challenges mirror gaps seen in comparable products, further confirming market demand for a solution like ours.
column28:
HyGear Gear 1
•	Bluetooth-enabled resistance bands that sync with the HYGEAR app
•	Tracks reps, resistance, time, and calories in real time
•	Compact and portable for general fitness
•	Limitations: Not designed for rehabilitation, lacks collaboration with physical therapy providers, and does not offer form correction or adaptive rehab planning

SmartWorkout Pro
•	Offers structured resistance band workouts with instructional videos
•	Clean, user-friendly app with a library of over 50 exercises
•	Limitations: No real-time exercise feedback, not integrated with smart resistance hardware, no partnerships with clinicians or rehab-specific workflows

LIT Axis
•	Smart resistance bands with Bluetooth sensors for live tracking
•	Offers personalized coaching, in-ear guidance, and performance metrics
•	Limitations: Primarily targets fitness enthusiasts, lacks partnerships with physical therapy clinics, and does not support clinician-directed rehabilitation plans

How Recura stands apart
•	Designed specifically for physical therapy patients, not general fitness users
•	Built for clinical integration, enabling therapists to monitor and adapt rehab plans
•	Offers real-time posture and resistance feedback to support safe recovery
•	Supports adaptive, personalized rehabilitation based on progress
•	Intuitive and easy to use in both clinical settings and at home
column29:
•	Developed the Recura iOS app, currently in beta, which allows users to log, summarize, and visualize physical therapy exercises
•	Designed core app features based on the FITT principle (Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type), with fields for sets, reps, duration, and exercise notes
•	Built out exercise tracking dashboards with user-friendly visualizations to support recovery insights and progress monitoring
•	Established clinical partnerships with The Physio Movement (Singapore) and Downtown Physical Therapy (Boston) for ongoing product testing and feedback
•	Began early user testing of the app in white-labeled form with patients from both clinical partners
column30:
•	Continue collaboration with existing clinical partners (The Physio Movement and Downtown Physical Therapy) while expanding to new physical therapy clinics for broader testing
•	Finalize and iterate on the smart resistance band prototype during the Fall semester using feedback from clinicians and early user trials
•	Integrate smart resistance band functionality directly into the Recura app for seamless data syncing, feedback, and progress tracking
•	Launch targeted pilot programs in Spring to place the device in the hands of physical therapy patients and clinics for real-world deployment
•	Continue work with Northeastern’s Venture Mentoring Network to explore VC funding and potential crowdfunding efforts for scaling Recura’s and our other app/hardware products.
column31:
This venture is deeply personal. I began developing Recura while undergoing intensive physical therapy following a severe shoulder injury and surgery. Collaborating directly with my physical therapists to shape the app has been transformative in my own recovery, and it’s my hope that Recura can offer others the same level of empowerment and clarity it gave me.

I'm also working with a team of fellow honors students through the Honors Propel Grant to build a broader ecosystem of wellness apps and hardware tools. We're excited about the opportunity to collaborate with Generate, and we're fully open to feedback, iteration, and creative input throughout the design and prototyping process.

column24: Affectrum Vault enhances medication adherence and self-awareness by seamlessly integrating a smart pill bottle with the Affectrum app, empowering individuals on medication to track, reflect, and stay accountable.
column25:
We seek Generate’s support in developing a prototype of a portable smart pill bottle with five programmable physical buttons—each mapped to configurable medication groupings via the Affectrum app. The device will house multiple internal compartments, each aligned to a preset. With a single press, it will dispense predefined doses from the appropriate compartments. Key features include:
•	LED feedback indicators for successful dispenses, errors, and battery/sync status
•	BLE or NFC connectivity with Affectrum’s app
•	USB-C charging and local dispense log storage
•	In-app customization and locking of presets
This tactile, intuitive design transforms routine adherence into a behaviorally satisfying, accountable process—one that resonates with Affectrum’s mission of demystifying mental health through seamless, data-driven tools.
column26: A successful outcome would include a fully functional smart pill bottle prototype capable of dispensing medication groupings from different programmable presets, each mapped to internal compartments. We’d also expect basic testing with users to confirm functionality and ergonomics, along with comprehensive documentation — including CAD files, circuit schematics, and API endpoints or BLE protocols for integration with our Affectrum app (which we’ll handle on our end). A successful partnership would result in a hardware system that's ready for field testing, refinement, and eventual production.
column27: Our primary target markets are individuals on complex medication or supplement regimens, as well as individuals managing mental health conditions. These users often struggle with staying consistent, organized, and emotionally attuned to their medication habits. Affectrum Vault addresses this by providing a satisfying, frictionless dispensing experience and syncing with the Affectrum app to visualize how medication intake correlates with mood, activity, and wellness trends. We've validated the need through feedback from Affectrum beta users and discussions with clinicians, which pointed to a strong demand for a hardware extension that reinforces accountability and behavioral insight.
column28:
•	AdhereTech – Enterprise-grade smart pill bottle with cellular tracking and adherence alerts; built for clinical trials and institutional care, not personalized wellness integration.
•	PillSafe – A new entrant focused on prescription safety, emphasizing secure dispensing and dose verification. While innovative for controlled substances, it lacks integration with emotional or behavioral health platforms.
•	Pillsy / TimerCap Bluetooth-cap-enabled pill bottles that track openings and send reminders, but offer no intelligent grouping, internal compartments, or data visualization.
•	MOBI Smart Dispenser / MedReady / MedMinder – Home-use dispensers with alarms and rotating compartments for elderly care—bulkier and designed more for compliance than user autonomy.
•	Hero Health – Automated pill dispenser that tracks refills and sends notifications. Robust for elderly or chronic care, but lacks app-based mood correlation or mobile-first design for young adults.

Why Affectrum Vault is different:
•	Preset-button logic + internal compartments allow for customizable routines and seamless user experience.
•	Built specifically to sync with the Affectrum app, creating a closed-loop system where users can see how medication aligns with mood, sleep, and daily behaviors.
•	Designed for mobile-first, mental health–focused individuals—offering intuitive UX, flexible scheduling, and the emotional intelligence most clinical tools lack.
column29:
•	Developed a fully functional iOS app (Affectrum) with NFC integration and substance/activity/mood tracking capabilities.
• Collaborated with service providers (mental wellness clinicians, therapists, etc.) on the development of a clinician-facing dashboard with data pulled from the Affectrum app, designed to visualize user mood and behavior data. Part of these conversations also outlined the need and foundation for future integration with Affectrum Vault to enhance remote medication monitoring and support.
column30:

Use momentum to enhance onboarding, security, and provider feedback systems.
column31:
Affectrum Vault is deeply personal. It’s not just a product, it’s a response to real struggles faced by people like me and many others trying to balance mental health, medication, and daily life. This project grew out of lived experience, guided by empathy and a drive to create something that genuinely makes managing wellness easier, smarter, and more human.

What excites me about working with Generate is the chance to collaborate with a team that values innovation, impact, and design.

column24: ClaimCrisis empowers disaster-affected families to quickly and accurately document lost or damaged belongings using AI-guided photo retrieval and reporting, making insurance claims faster, easier, and more successful.
column25: We would like Generate to build a streamlined web and mobile platform that guides users through our 20-question claim assistant, securely scans their camera roll for relevant pre-disaster photos, and generates a polished, insurer-ready report with image-based proof and estimated values of their claimable items. This contributes to our core value by automating the most time-consuming and error prone step in the claim process. This enables families to quickly submit accurate documentation of their claimable items, so that they can maximize their payouts from their insurance companies.
column26: At the end of the semester with Generate, we would consider it a success to have a fully functional, user-friendly product that allows us to onboard real users, families, relief organizations, and insurance partners, and begin testing and delivering value in live disaster recovery situations. Having a tool we can confidently market and deploy into production would be a huge milestone toward proving product-market fit and our efforts to scale our impact.
column27:
Our target market includes disaster-affected families and individuals, particularly those impacted by events like wildfires, hurricanes, or floods, who need to file insurance claims but struggle to document what was lost. We’re also targeting relief organizations and smaller insurance companies that support these families and would benefit from a faster, more reliable claims process.
To begin testing product-market fit, we’ve conducted early interviews and discovery conversations with disaster survivors, including individuals affected by natural disasters, such as Hurricane Sandy, Hurricane Harvey.  We have also gained informal feedback from caseworkers and peers in the emergency response space, such as nurses and EMT’s. These conversations confirmed that recalling and proving item loss is a major pain point, validating the need for a tool like Claim Crisis. We're now preparing to launch a prototype so we can continue testing with real users and iterate based on their experiences.
column28:
Traditional Insurance Company Claim Portals

- Why it's a competitor: Most major insurers like State Farm, Allstate, and Progressive have internal online claim systems.

- Limitation: These systems often place the burden on the user to remember and manually list all lost items, with little to no visual or AI support.

- How Claim Crisis is different: We use AI to scan users’ camera rolls and provide visual proof, significantly reducing user effort and increasing claim accuracy.

Public Adjusters

- Why it's a competitor: Public adjusters help homeowners compile claims and negotiate with insurance companies.

- Limitation: They are often expensive, work slowly, and may not be accessible to low-income families or in rural areas.

- How Claim Crisis is different: We provide a fast, affordable, and scalable tech-based solution that users can access immediately after a disaster.

Home Inventory Apps (e.g., Sortly, Encircle, HomeZada)

- Why it's a comparable product: These apps help users keep track of belongings for insurance purposes.

- Limitation: Most require users to catalog items in advance—something very few people actually do.

column24: ReachApply transforms the job search for students by uniting AI-powered resume tailoring, automated outreach, and real-time contact discovery—turning scattered cold applications into targeted, high-impact workflows that get results.
column25:
We would love Generate to help us ship a robust, production-ready version of ReachApply that connects the dots across job discovery, contact management, and AI-powered outreach. Specifically, we’re seeking support with:

- A dynamic job + contact dashboard that lets users segment contacts into live “campaigns” (e.g., “Investment Banking – JP Morgan”) and track engagement.

- Real-time job drop detection, surfacing newly posted roles (e.g., “Bank of America just launched 2026 internships”) and matching them to the user’s preferences.

- Backend logic to support job-contact linking, so once you apply to a job, the system immediately suggests relevant people at that company to message.

- Fully integrated AI resume + cover letter tailoring per job.

- Smart outreach tooling to instantly send messages, schedule follow-ups, and track replies—either through the Chrome extension or the dashboard.

- This build-out would directly power our core value: helping students move faster, stand out earlier, and never miss an opportunity.
column26:
Success would be a live, end-to-end MVP that integrates all 3 layers of the platform:

1. Real-Time Job Discovery
   - Job drop feed tied to user’s goals (e.g., “Bain just posted a new role”).
   - One-click tailoring of resume and CL to apply immediately.

2. Dashboard-Based Contact Campaigns
   - User can create segmented dashboards (e.g., “Private Equity – NYC”) and add contacts from the Chrome extension or bulk import.
   - Tags by company, title, reply status, and follow-up history.

3. Integrated Outreach + Tracker
   - AI-generated messages from job-contact pairs
   - Gmail/Outlook OAuth to send directly
   - Campaign analytics (open, reply, conversion rates)

With this, we’ll be able to launch an alpha with 50–100 students and collect live usage data for pilots and funding.
column27:
Target Market:
   - Undergraduate and international students applying to internships and early-career jobs—especially in competitive industries like consulting, finance, and tech.
   - Students who rely heavily on LinkedIn networking, cold outreach, and resume tailoring to secure interviews.

Product-Market Fit Testing:
   - While we haven’t launched a working product yet, we’ve validated our direction through 30+ interviews with students from Northeastern, Cornell, Duke, and NYU.
   - We’ve identified strong pain points—especially around resume fatigue, ghosted cold emails, and missed job drops.
   - An early interest form collected 75+ signups from students eager to test a smarter job-seeking tool.
column28:
Teal HQ – Teal helps users track job applications and store materials in one place. It’s visually clean and student-friendly, but fundamentally passive—it doesn’t support live job discovery, resume personalization, or outreach. ReachApply fills that gap by actively sourcing jobs, tailoring resumes with AI, and connecting users to contacts who can actually move the needle.

RecruitU – RecruitU offers curated job boards and school-specific career tools, often through university partnerships. While it surfaces relevant roles, the platform stops short of enabling action—it doesn’t support resume tailoring, contact discovery, or integrated outreach. There’s no way to message anyone or track responses. ReachApply goes beyond job discovery by letting users email relevant contacts directly, launch outreach campaigns, and organize their follow-ups—all from one place.


WonsultingAI / ResumAI – These tools focus exclusively on resume generation using AI. While helpful for one-off edits, they lack context from the job description or a feedback loop tied to application outcomes. We take it further—tailoring both resumes and cover letters to individual job posts, storing those versions, and linking them to outreach campaigns.


Huntr – Huntr’s value lies in its Kanban-style application tracking, which many students love. However, it stops short of guiding next steps—there’s no intelligent resume editing, no contact scraping, and no actionable outreach layer. ReachApply is built around helping you take the next step, not just track the last one.


Apollo.io – Apollo is a best-in-class platform for scraping contacts and sending outreach campaigns—if you’re a salesperson. It’s not built for job-seekers, doesn’t handle resumes, and requires manual work to filter useful contacts. We’ve adapted the power of Apollo’s backend and layered it with a job-seeker’s workflow—automated email drafts, tagging by company/role, and a CRM for referrals, not cold leads.


Simplify Jobs – Simplify shines when it comes to fast applications—users can autofill forms in one click. But the trade-off is quality: there’s no resume tailoring, no insight into which applications worked, and no human connection. ReachApply keeps that speed, but adds depth: customized documents, real-time job alerts, and contact-based follow-through.


LinkedIn Easy Apply – Every student uses it. Few succeed with it. Easy Apply’s strength is convenience, but it encourages a spray-and-pray approach that leads to burnout and ghosting. ReachApply flips the model by prompting strategic outreach, offering targeted job drops, and helping users stand out instead of blend in.

column31:
We’ve lived the reality of job hunting as students—reaching out to dozens of people on LinkedIn, sending cold emails, trying to land coffee chats and referrals just to get noticed. It’s a process that works, but it’s incredibly time-consuming, hard to organize, and easy to lose track of who responded, who referred you, and what follow-up is due.

That’s where we started: building something that could make networking easier and smarter—automating the outreach, tracking responses, and organizing contacts by company or industry. But we didn’t want to stop there.

We realized that applying to jobs was just as broken. Every role requires a new resume, a new cover letter, and you spend hours tailoring documents that often go unseen. It’s exhausting and inefficient.

So we’re building ReachApply to solve both sides of the equation: smarter outreach and streamlined applications—automated, personalized, and trackable, all in one place. And we’re excited to build it with a team like Generate that understands how technical solutions can truly change outcomes.

column25: I would like Generate to complete a first-pass prototype of the filament recycler to crush/shred old or failed 3D prints, followed by melting and respooling into usable rolls of filament - all while keeping user safety and manufacturability in mind during the design process.
column26: There are two exit pathways that I envision based on the performance of the prototype by showcase. If the prototype is functional, I would love to donate it to the sherm makerspace for use by all of the Sherman programs. From this use, I would ask users for their feedback about future improvements and feasibility to be scaled. column27: The target market is makerspaces with many users, such as university makerspaces or other community makerspaces, and higher volume print farms. This wouldn’t be designed for individual users since they wouldn’t produce enough filament waste to make the cost of this product worthwhile. I haven’t tested the market fit yet, though I will perform testing following the development of this product.
column28: There is a hobbyist version of a filament recycler called ARTME 3D, which can be seen in this YouTube video: https://youtu.be/BT04glGDjB4?si=FWjMaHqjnzTtaIWL. This is primarily designed for individual users and requires the user to print most of the components themselves and manually assemble the machine. This type of machine wouldn’t work well, however, since it’s intended for individual users whose scale of filament waste is too low, and the assembly time requires skilled users and takes time to properly assemble. Additionally, Loop is another competitor that plans to achieve similar goals to the filament recycler I envision, however the price that it is set at is much higher than is reasonable, and it also hasn’t been released yet - and many are skeptical about it’s functionality. A video about this product can be found here: https://youtu.be/GV7y1FnxAVE?si=nSk6S8-xh8OW0CZu
column29: I haven’t made any progress toward this product yet, so there is mostly a blank slate to work with.
column30: Once the prototype is functional, either entirely from generate or with a little further development on my own, I will perform user testing in the sherm makerspace. From this, I will first learn if this is a viable product that should be pursued further. If it is a worthwhile product, I would then compile a list of improvements from the testing and continue to iteratively develop the product and collaborate with manufacturers to determine scalability options.
column31: For the scope of a generate project, this would specifically be focusing on recycling PLA filament only. Ideally the user flow would be to place the old or failed print into a hopper/container that would then be crushed/shredded into smaller PLA pellets that would then be passed through a heating chamber and extruder where the filament could then be automatically respooled with high precision (consistent filament diameter is important to prevent clogs). This product would be fully assembled when purchased, not requiring the user to manually print, assemble, and calibrate their own machines. Electrically, I’m flexible to work at the complexity generate wants to work at. I personally think it would be best to use dev boards that go into headers on the PCB so that the product can go through rigorous testing and iteration early, but if it’s a better learning opportunity to do more fun stuff like onboard microcontroller chips and boot loading, then that’s also great. My main goal for this generate project is to be a fun and rewarding learning experience that can help make the makerspace more efficient! Also if it’s more convenient to do the interview in person at the sherm, I’m definitely available to do so!

It would be a mobile app (or web app if easier) that allows users to input their preferences (type of music, area, etc) and receive a customized night-out plan with 2 to 3 suggested spots with a timeline of when to start where. The app would ideally present a starting bar, a second stop, and a club or final destination, along with short descriptions of each place.

We would also love basic filters (eg. chill vs energetic, large group vs date night) and a clean layout. We care a lot about making this app easy and fun to use. The user experience should be smooth and inviting, not a list of places you could Google yourself. We would love help designing a simple and clear interface that makes users feel excited about their night. The idea is to turn the messy, last-minute ‘where do we go’ stress into a smooth experience that feels tailored to the user.

column27:
Target: 
Collect students and young adults in Boston (around ages 20-30) who like going out but are tired of guessing where to go and wasting money on places that don’t match their vibe.

Tested: 
We’ve talked to many friends and almost everyone says the same thing: unless they’ve lived somewhere for years, they never really know where to go out. People usually rely on Tiktok, Google, or word of mouth and it is usually hit or miss. This is especially true when you’re visiting a new city; if you don’t do a ton of research or have friends there, it is really hard to figure out a fun night plan.
column28:
Discotech: A nightlife app that lets users discover and book club events, festivals, and nightlife experiences in major cities in the U.S. and some internationally. It provides features like free guest list access, ticket purchasing, and VIP table reservations directly through the app. Geared towards young adults who go to clubs and music events.
Joyraft: A mobile app for finding local events and nightlife experiences, originally launched in Boston. Highlights unique outings, showing users experiences they don’t want to miss around town. Aimed at 20-30 year olds planning fun nights out with friends. It offers curated event listings, you can see what your friends are interested in, and sends alerts about local events.
Bandsintown: App and platform that helps users discover concerts near them and notifies you of upcoming gigs by artists you listen to. Targeted at concert-goers and allows you to track favorite artists, find local concerts and festivals, and get ticket info. The app has tens of millions of users globally.
DICE: A mobile ticketing app that ‘makes going out easy’ by helping users discover and attend the best live shows, club nights, and festivals nearby. DICE offers personalized event recommendations and you can connect your Spotify to get tailored picks. You can follow friends/artists and they have quick-in app ticket purchases with transparent pricing. Originally from the UK, but now operates in major cities in North America and Europe.
Eventbrite: A well-known event discovery and ticketing platform that has nightlife and other hobby events. Users can browse local happenings, get personalized recommendations, and buy tickets in-app. Eventbrite is available in cities worldwide and appeals to a broad audience.
Tablelist: A nightlife reservations app focused on VIP clubbing experiences. Tablelist provides access to VIP bottle service, private tables, and exclusive events at nightclubs in a growing number of cities. Aimed at upscale, young adults who enjoy club nights.
BarChick: A global bar and club discovery app that showcases the best nightlife spots BarChick’s writers create guides to top bars and late-night venues in more than 50 cities around the world. The app provides information on each bar’s vibe, highlights ‘last-minute’ party-spots and includes reviews/tips for a great night out. Geared toward bar-hoppers and travelers in their 20s and 30s.
InList: A members-only nightlife app that curates exclusive parties and high-end events across the globe. Keeps users in the know in about 45 cities and caters to an upscale demographic of late-20s/30s professionals and travelers. It offers VIP access to trendy clubs, parties, and celebrity events.
TikTok: Of course TikTok isn’t a dedicated nightlife app, but the platform indirectly competes in the nightlife space by influencing where young adults choose to go. Many people in their 20s and 30s discover trending bars, clubs, and events via viral TikTok videos and influencer recommendations.The content often drives its user’s nightlife decisions, making it an indirect competitor.

Each of these platforms is actively operating and targets Nightinerary’s same core audience of young adults looking for fun nights out. They are solutions that help users find nightlife experiences, whether through curated events, social recommendations, or easy booking tools.

column28:
Modern Life – A tech-forward life insurance brokerage with some quoting and CRM tools, but lacks embedded AI workflows and is not designed for agent-led case design and underwriting support.

Praxos AI – An AI tool for insurance professionals focused on automation of proposals and comparisons, but not deeply integrated into underwriting or policy placement workflows.

Qumis – A rising gen AI platform for insurance professionals. Focuses on text-based answers and RAG pipelines but lacks a robust document workspace or pre-underwriting tools. Strong UI, but early-stage on workflow integration.

Harvey AI – A leading verticalized AI platform in legal and finance. Offers deep domain-specific LLM capabilities and shows the power of specialized copilots — but is focused on law, not insurance distribution.

Bestow / Quility – Full-stack life insurance platforms focusing on direct-to-consumer or embedded agent distribution. They streamline parts of the journey but don’t serve the producer’s full workflow or offer customizable AI tools.

ChatGPT / Claude / Gemini – General-purpose LLMs used by some advisors for research or writing. Lacks fine-tuning, regulation awareness, or integration into insurance systems — requiring heavy manual prompting.

iPipeline / Advisor360 / Salesforce – Industry incumbents with deep adoption but poor UX and limited AI capabilities. They serve back-office and carrier needs better than frontline producers.

- Instagram fan pages / Twitter spaces: Real-time discussion, but not archived and community-owned or driven

While I'm a first-time founder, I believe I bring transferrable skills through my background studying Business Administration, my competitive experience and accolades in public speaking, my time working in consulting, finance, and project management, and my creativity as an artist, musician, and lover of cinema.

column24: A platform where users can discover, share, and engage with the funniest and most interesting social media comments—all in one place.
column25: Coding

column27: Our target market is Gen Z and Millennials who actively browse social media platforms like YouTube, Twitter, and TikTok and enjoy sharing and engaging with viral, humorous, or thought-provoking content.
column28: Some comparable products include Reddit, Instagram meme pages, iFunny, 9GAG, and niche communities like the “Comment Awards” subreddit. Reddit allows users to upvote and interact with content, but it’s not focused on curating humorous or interesting comments from other platforms. Instagram meme pages and similar accounts share screenshots of viral comments, but they’re limited to passive viewing with no way for users to upload or organize content themselves. Platforms like iFunny and 9GAG focus on general humor, but they don’t highlight social media comment culture specifically. Meanwhile, communities like “Comment Awards” do celebrate funny comments but are restricted to Reddit and lack a dedicated, standalone experience. Our product fills this gap by offering a focused, community-driven space where users can actively share and engage with the best comments from across the internet.
